description: Achlys triphylla, with the common names deer foot, vanilla leaf, or sweet after death, is a plant species native to the mountains of the West Coast of North America.
origin: ORE, WAS, WESTERN CANADA, SOUTHWESTERN USA, WAS OO

care.tips: Native to: NORTHWESTERN USA, WAS, BRC OO, BRC, CAL

description: Berberis amurensis, commonly known as Amur barberry, is a shrub native to Japan, Korea, the Russian Far East, and parts of China. It is named for the Amur River, which forms part of the boundary between Russia and China. It is found at elevations of 1100–2900 m.
origin: CHN SA, JAP HN, CHS HN, CHS AH, CHI NX

care.tips: Native to: CHN SX, EASTERN ASIA, CHM JL, CHN SD, JAP HK | Similar plants: Berberis gracilis
common_problems: Check plants from April to October, and if you spot an infestation, organic and non-organic sprays are available, but tend to be more effective on young larvae
"}},{"type":"highlight-box","data":{"content":"
common_pests: Look out for small caterpillars that are the larvae of the berberis sawfly
These can quickly strip a plant of its leaves, which can be devastating for a berberis hedge
Check plants from April to October, and if you spot an infestation, organic and non-organic sprays are available, but tend to be more effective on young larvae
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) The wood is a source of yellow dye. The fruits and roots of some species are used medicinally. The fruits of some species are used for jellies and wine.
care.edible_parts: The fruits of some species are edible.
care.pruning_info: Pruning a berberis hedge. Sarah Cuttle Prune evergreen or semi-evergreen berberis lightly once a year to maintain their shape. Formal hedges can be pruned twice a year. Bear in mind that, if cut back after flowering, shrubs will not produce berries, so trim in winter if you want to keep the fruit. Prune deciduous berberis in winter, when plants are leafless and dormant. Most varieties need minimal pruning, just to keep their shape, but variegated varieties benefit from a harder prune, to enhance
care.propagation_detail: Propagate berberis by taking semi-ripe cuttings in late summer or early autumn.

description: Deep dusty blue/black edible globose berries in clusters give excellent fall interest. They are less than an inch in size.
origin: South West Canada to Northern California
synonyms: ["Berberis aquifolium var. juglandifolia", "Berberis aquifolium var. lyallii", "Berberis pinnata var. hortensis", "Mahonia aquifolium var. nutkana", "Mahonia latifolia", "Mahonia murrayana", "Berberis brevipes", "Mahonia aquifolium var. juglandifolia", "Odostemon nutkanus", "Mahonia pinnata var. hortensis"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Dappled Sunlight (Shade through upper canopy all day) | Climate: temperate
care.tips: Similar plants: Berberis bealei | Native to: NSW, BGM BE, MNT OO, FRA FR, UKR MO
common_problems: Leaf spots and rusts may occur
Improperly sited plants may suffer from leaf scorch
Deer tend to avoid this plant

Uses

Best in small groupings in shady areas
Shrub borders
common_pests: Watch for aphids, scale and whiteflies
care.used_for: ["Berries make jelly", "wine or purple dye; inner bark is used as a yellow dye."]
care.used_for_details: Berries make jelly, wine or purple dye; inner bark is used as a yellow dye.
care.edible_parts: Not poisonous, but berries contain berberine that can cause stomach upset, especially with children.

synonyms: ["Mahonia knightii", "Berberis knightii", "Berberis costulata", "Berberis darwinii var. pendula", "Berberis darwinii var. magellanica"]

description: After flowering, berry-like seeds appear, 1/3 of an inch across. These seeds are initially green, but they later become bright blue at maturity during the summer. The seed coat is fleshy. Because of the bright blue seed coats, woodland birds disperse the seeds, which are known to be toxic to humans.
origin: CHN SA, JAP HN, CHS HN, CHS AH, CHI NX

care.tips: Native to: CHN SX, EASTERN ASIA, CHM JL, CHN SD, JAP HK | Similar plants: Podophyllum peltatum

description: In the fall, this plant produces bright red, round berries on large terminal panicles that persist through the winter. The berries are 8 mm in diameter. Each berry contains two seeds.
origin: Central and Southern China and Japan

care.light_guide: NC State: Dappled Sunlight (Shade through upper canopy all day)
care.toxicity_note: Toxic to Dogs, Toxic to Cats, Toxic to Horses. Signs: Weakness, incoordination, seizures, coma, respiratory failure, death (rare in pets).
common_problems: This shrub has no serious insect or disease problems.
Because heavenly bamboo is listed as invasive by the NC Invasive Plant Council and is toxic if ingested by pets or children, please see the alternatives listed to the left.
Consider planting  Fire Power  or 'Gulf Stream', which are sterile (non-fruiting) cultivars of heavenly bamboo, or perhaps an ornamental shrub native to North Carolina, such as the
common_pests: This shrub has no serious insect or disease problems.
care.toxicity_symptoms: Weakness, incoordination, seizures, coma, respiratory failure, death (rare in pets).
care.toxicity_first_aid: Remove any remaining plant material from mouth. Rinse with water. Monitor for symptoms (vomiting, drooling, lethargy). Call poison control or vet if symptoms appear.

description: Podophyllum is a genus of flowering plant in the family Berberidaceae, native from Afghanistan to China, and from southeast Canada to the central and eastern United States. The genus was first described by Carl Linnaeus in 1753.
origin: South East Canada to Central   Southeastern U.S.A

care.light_guide: NC State: Deep shade (Less than 2 hours to no direct sunlight)
care.toxicity_note: Toxic to Dogs, Toxic to Cats, Toxic to Horses. Signs: Vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, panting, coma (rare); dermal - redness, skin ulcers.
common_problems: The Mayapple has no serious insect or disease problems.
Yellow or orange fungal rust may infect the leaves.
common_pests: The Mayapple has no serious insect or disease problems.
care.toxicity_symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, panting, coma (rare); dermal - redness, skin ulcers.
care.toxicity_first_aid: Remove any remaining plant material from mouth. Rinse with water. Monitor for symptoms (vomiting, drooling, lethargy). Call poison control or vet if symptoms appear.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) Native Americans used this plant for medicinal purposes.
care.edible_parts:
EDIBLE PARTS: Ripe (yellow and soft) fruit is edible raw but in limited quantity. The fruits may be used to make jellies and preserves. The flavor is said to be bland and somewhat like an overripe melon.

CAUTION: The roots, seeds, and leaves are poisonous. Do NOT eat. HARVEST TIME: Collect in Aug
